description: This bar chart displays the corrosion rate (unit: mm/y) of four Fe-Mn-Al-C low-density steel (LDS) samples after 30 days of immersion in 3.5 wt% NaCl solution. The tested samples include: S0 (as-hot-rolled without subsequent solution treatment, with non-uniform grain size, κ-carbide precipitates and high dislocation density), S950 (solution treated at 950 ℃ for 2 h then water quenched, fine average grain size 8.7 μm), S1050 (solution treated at 1050 ℃ for 2 h then water quenched, medium grain size), and S1150 (solution treated at 1150 ℃ for 2 h then water quenched, coarse average grain size 32 μm). Measured corrosion rates are 0.326 mm/y for S0, 0.160 mm/y for S950, 0.248 mm/y for S1050, and 0.281 mm/y for S1150. The trend shows that grain refinement via lower-temperature solution treatment drastically reduces corrosion rate, with the finest-grained S950 sample exhibiting the lowest corrosion rate (43% lower than coarse-grained S1150). This figure supports the core conclusion that optimized grain size promotes formation of protective Al-rich oxide films, significantly improving corrosion resistance of LDSs in chloride-containing environments.
